twitter_thread: 63% of people can't cover a $500 emergency. Those who can, save $1,378/month on average.
The 50/30/20 rule works: 50% for necessities like rent ($1,500/month), 30% for discretionary spending ($900/month), 20% for saving ($600/month)
Qapital's automated savings tool can move $500/month into a separate account, earning 1.5% interest
Paying off a $2,000 credit card balance with 18% interest saves $360/year in interest payments
Ibotta's cashback app can add $200/year to your savings, just for buying groceries
Directing a 3% raise towards savings instead of lifestyle inflation can add $1,000/year to your emergency fund
What's the most effective way you've found to build an emergency fund? #personalfinance #financialfreedom
pack.linkedin: 63% of people can't cover a $500 emergency expense, but those who can, save an average of $1,378/month. They often follow the 50/30/20 budget rule, allocating 50% for necessities, 30% for discretionary spending, and 20% for saving. Tools like Qapital can automate $500/month in savings, while paying off high-interest debt can save $360/year. What strategies have you found most effective in building your own emergency fund?
